Hey everyone, this is Russell Brunson. Welcome back to the 10X Secrets Closing Masterclass. And this interview is one that I am so excited to share with you. It's with one of my close friends named Jason Flattelin. And Jason's one of the few people, I think, who geeks out on marketing and sales and psychology as much as I do. It's really hard to find people who are as obsessed with it as me. And every time I talk to Jason, it's like we can go in these rabbit holes for hours. Talking about selling and persuasion, psychology, how it works, and all these amazing things. And so I'm excited because you guys have a chance to listen on a conversation we had going deep into this as it relates back to webinars and closing on webinars. Now, a couple cool things you'll see in this interview. Number one is that the first thing we talk about is how Jason actually uses his webinars, the way he does his webinars, to become one of the highest-paid super affiliates in the world. In fact, during one campaign, he'll share the actual numbers. But I would say my guess is I think that the ClickFunnels webinar has probably made more money than any webinar in the history of the world. And his webinar has made more money as an affiliate than anyone in the history of the world. And so it's kind of cool. You're hearing from two different perspectives, mine, which is like, here's how to do it if you are the product owner. Jason's like, you don't have to even be the product owner. You can still use webinars to crush sales. So for any of you guys who are like, I don't have a product yet. I have an idea. I don't have anything yet. You don't have to have one. Jason will show you how he used webinars to sell other people's products to the tune of tens of millions of dollars. It is insanely cool. So we start the conversation there. And we start transitioning over into all these other cool, different paths and things that I wanted to talk to him about. We talk about breaking belief patterns. In fact, I learned that originally from Jason. I used to always just do my webinar or my presentation where I would like intro and then I would like stack and close. In the middle, I would just talk about whatever I wanted. And Jason was one that helped me understand how do you find their false beliefs? You break them, then you reframe them. And what's interesting is that the way he does it is different than mine. In fact, we kind of geek out on this in the interview. I'm like, this is how I would do it if it was me and he's like, whoa. He's like, I use the same concept, but completely different. This is how I use it. And some of you guys might be freaking out, like, which one's right? Russell, those are Jason's. And the answer is they're both right. You can learn things from both sides of it. We also go deep into how to actually create an offer. Like how do you create a product and bonuses? And like, how do you create bonuses that are so valuable that people freak out and have to give you money? We went deep into that. You're probably thinking this, right? You're probably thinking this, right? And just breaking down all the false belief patterns for as long as people will allow you to do it. Never been done before. I don't know if I'll ever be able to do it again, but $25 million through webinars for somebody else's product. It doesn't make any logical sense. I remember watching those campaigns happening from the back, I was up in the bleachers watching this, and I'm just like, I don't even know how he's doing that. It's insane. And I remember we were at a genius network the first time we kinda really connected and talked, and I was like, how did you do that? And you're like, well the cool thing is that there's like, because of how much money was involved, I was able to use not just one or two tricks, but I was able to use all the tricks. You start telling me all this stuff, and I was just like, oh my gosh, this is insanely cool. So I'm hoping to get some of that stuff from you today. But, I guess let me start that, because a lot of people don't realize you can use these things not just for your own products, but as an affiliate. What was your mindset going into that, or what were you thinking when you were like, I'm gonna be an affiliate and use webinars and everything else to actually crush this launch and to do it over and over and over again? Yeah, that's a great question Russell, because the reality to me is most of the time I have found the webinar to be the most effective tool for making sales. And if that's the case, then we work backwards. We say, where are webinars not being used at all, or are they not being properly utilized? And in the affiliate space, the opportunity there is most people rely on the vendors pitches, the vendors landing pages, the vendors sales, and all that kinda stuff, their angles. But the problem is, if you're competing with a hundred other affiliates and you're all using the same material, what advantage do you have? So when we could do a webinar, where we were the only ones on the webinar, we don't even bring the product owner on the webinar. And we have the ability to say things and sell and send them to places that no other affiliate can. That's like the tiny little difference that makes all the difference. So that's why we like that, because we're like, a, webinars are pretty effective. We can't use it out of the box like you would normally use a webinar the way most people are used to doing it. But if we tweak it a little bit, if we try a couple different things, what's the worst that can happen? We strike out and then we go back to the drawing board, but the best that can happen is, that's when you truly have game-changing results. When you take something that nobody else is doing, apply it properly, and you just, it's unfair. It's like playing T-ball with a four-year-olds. Ha ha ha ha. Well, it's crazy about it. I remember when you were doing this campaign, that first it was like, you were doing the webinar and you were crushing it, and you were making so much more money on your webinar than people were making, sending traffic to the vendors sales pages. Then you started getting all the affiliates to promote your webinar in exchange for like half the commissions, right? Something crazy like that, when you're like, I'll just close the people for you. I'll give you half the commission, I keep half, but we're still gonna make more than if you were to send it directly to the, to the, to the, to the, to the, to the, to the, That's exactly right. I mean, and it's brilliant if you think about it, because I have to double your conversion rate for you to make the same amount of money. So that's not an easy thing to convince another affiliate. But again, if you have your own sales vehicle, you have something that they can promote that nobody else can promote now besides them. And so in most cases, this was the pitch Russell, I'm sure you'll appreciate this. I'd go to them and say, hey, listen, I can't guarantee I'll double your sales or more, which I'd have to do for you to make the same amount of money, but I can guarantee this is the least work you'll ever do in an affiliate promotion, and your audience will get the best bonus package that they could ever hope to get. And then, oh yeah, by the way, four out of five times, we triple conversion, but I can't guarantee that. I can't guarantee it was probably gonna happen. You was fascinating is, I wanna go deeper on the affiliate stuff because I know a lot of people watching this don't have a product yet. They're like, oh, this sounds awesome Russell, but I don't have a product. We, I think you know, we're working on building out a speaking team right now for ClickFunnel so that I can stay with my kids and send other people out on the road. And we have like 250 people apply for the speaking team, then 50 you submit webinars. And there was this one guy who like didn't make the cut. Instead of like being like, oh, I'm Mope, he's like, you know what, I'm just gonna build my own webinar and sell ClickFunnel. So he wrote his own webinar pitch, started promoting it to sell ClickFunnels. And in the next like two months, he won a dream car, got tons of affiliate commissions, all of him making his own webinar pitching ClickFunnels without me involved. Like just, he just did it, or selling it and I was making tons of money. And so like, you don't have to have your own product to make money with webinars. Like, is you proven, is this guy proven? It's just really powerful. Now, what I would love to talk about, cause I want to get deeper into closing and since some of, cause you're one of the most brilliant like marketing psychologists of all time, like being able to look at human brain and I want to get deep into that. But before we do, just for again, just kind of add on with people who don't have a product yet. Can you talk about how you, you took like, here's the person's product, it was Amazon selling machines, right? It was Amazon selling machines, course and product you were selling. When you came back and you just say, I'm selling this course, you created an offer around it that was better than everybody else's offer, which is why people literally bought the course and then came bought it again a second time through you because they wanted, you increase the value of the offer so much so they wanted to buy it twice just to get your offer plus however they bought it before. So I think that's a thing that a lot of people are missing is just like, if there were to be a webinar for this, they click thumbs, they would just sell click thumbs where I do it. It's like, no, you can create something more amazing and just talk about that a little bit. Yeah, so the idea here becomes, first of all, the greatest word in advertising is free. You're not going to beat that. So free is so powerful. But what's funny is you can get away with it psychologically speaking of selling something for free. You're like, wait a minute, that doesn't make sense. But here's the brilliant thing is when you create a bonus package and make it exclusive. So that's ultimate scarcity, right? When you make the bonus package exclusive, you say, you can't buy this from me at any price. I won't allow you to pay me money for this. However, you can get it for free if you buy this other guy's package. And so you get double duty. You get something that is so incredible, so powerful because you can pick and choose where you want a bonus stack because you're not starting from a blank slate. You're looking at a finished product and you're standing on the shoulder of a giant essentially. And then again, the real trick here is making it exclusive, making it something that they cannot get anywhere else even if they tried to pay for it. And so you get the double benefit, the benefit of it being free, right? And the benefit of it being positioned to where you're selling, 95% of our pitch was not about their product. This is a $3,500 product. We would spend four slides talking about their product and then we'd spend about 127 slides talking about our bonuses. You get to focus on the free thing and sell the free thing, right? And that's where the brilliance of this positioning lies. Okay, can you give me some examples? So I think, wasn't Amazon selling machines like four grand at a time? It was a training course and how to use Amazon. So give me examples of stuff that you created to be able to make that exclusive bonus that you used when you were selling this. Yeah, so because there was such a high profit margin in there, one of the things that we did is we found a really good coach and we say, listen, we'll pay you $300 every single time you work with our clients. So they get three one-on-one sessions. So they get a product selection session. They get like a listing tweaking session and they get some other session. I can't even remember what it is right now. And we could pay him $300. We obviously are making bank off the rest of the selling but the stack in terms of the bonus to the client is unbelievable. And then the more you do it, the better it becomes because pretty soon we had about 70 testimonials of these people talking about how great this Amazon coach is. And we tied up an exclusive with them. You couldn't hire him as a coach. You couldn't pay him as a coach, right? But you could get it for free. And here's how I positioned it, Russell, because I think this is important because the positioning is just as important as the bonus. I would say, and this is how I out-positioned everybody else in the promotion. I'd say, really, I go, if any coach is willing to do coaching for you for free, how good are they? Like a really good coach, you're not gonna get for free. So instead of you paying the coach, I'll just pay for you. Now, but here's what's brilliant about it. That's baked into the ticket, the final price, so the product, right? So it's still free to them, but it has premium position and as if they're paying top dollar for the top coach. Whereas all these other people promoting the course were like, I'll do the coaching for you for free. And then the positioning to the customer is like, well, you're desperate trying to sell it and you're giving away your time for free, so how good are you? Where we're essentially engineering a situation where we can look better than everybody else, give a better solution, because we truly were paying this coach. And then as a benefit to that as an outcome, we were able to then figure out from that is let's make this guy a group coach. And so then he started doing the one-on-one coaching and the group coaching. So we would then throw them in a two-month program for the group coaching for Amazon. So they'd get on and get a couple sessions because our positioning there was, hey, Amazon's changing all the time. So if you buy a course, you get it up to the minute in that minute for what happens next month or the month after that. And so now you have, it always is refreshing and updating it from an affiliate perspective where it isn't from the vendor perspective. So again, that's just a really powerful way to position a bonus and do some financial arbitrage. Because if I'm making $1,800 to $2,000 a sale, I would very happily pay somebody else $2 or $300 if it just increases my conversion by two or three or four or 5%. Yeah. I think one of the things you're brilliant at is how you look at positioning. In fact, last time me and you talked like this in this office through the cameras like this was like a week before I spoke at the TenX event. And I had my slides all done and you shared one little nugget to me. This is when we got off, I freaked out, went to my slides, I tweaked it and it crushed it so strong at the event. So those who have watched the TenX Seekers training already, remember when I get to the stack and one of my bonuses was traffic secrets. And the way I had it before is like, hey, and I bought this course in John Reese called Traffic Seekers, she has to get it for free. And you told me you're like, one of the bonuses you had, I think it was a tray or something, you wired him money to interview him and Tray was like, no, I'll do it for free. You're like, no, I have to wire you the money because I want to show a picture of the wire. So there's the actual value. And you said that, I was like, I literally wired John Reese almost a million dollars to buy traffic secrets. So I went to my, we got hung up, I ran into my CP over here, I'm like, do you get screenshots of all the wires we ever sent to John Reese, see all the screenshots. And then in my slide deck, I had all the screenshots. And so I got up and I said, this is Traffic Seekers and John's company made a million bucks an hour, I bought it from him. Back here, I wired him a million dollars so I could give this to you for free. How many of you want it for free? And that's like going crazy. I was like, hey, this costs me literally a million dollars. You guys get it for free today if you invest in the thing and they were all nuts and went crazy for it. And you don't sell traffic secrets on its own, right Russell? No, we don't. No, cause the moment you put a price tag on that, say it's a thousand or $2,000, now it's a $2,000 bonus for free. But when you say this is so important for your success that I went out and got it for you, now the value is a million dollars or whatever you paid for it and it's irrefutable proof cause you can show it. So instead of potential value, it's actual value and that audience is thinking, oh my God, you've invested in me a million dollars just to give me something for free and you're only asking me to invest with what, two grand with you, three grand with you? That seems like a fair deal. Yeah. So I love talking cause it always goes down in rabbit holes, I'm not expecting, but I don't want to stop cause this is so good from like a positioning standpoint. I hope everyone is paying attention. Cause a lot of times they're like, and my third bonus is this ebook I wrote about how to, and it's just like, oh, I'm like, no one's buying my thing, Russell. It's like, God, cause your positioning of the things are not right. Like what are the things, like when you're, when you're creating the positioning on each part of the bonus or the offer, things like that, what are other things that are going through your head that you're trying to figure out as you're doing this? Yeah, that's a great question. So the first rule that we attest by is if the bonus doesn't have an overwhelming, compelling, oh my God factor, it will actually hurt the sale, not help the sale. So I'll give you an example. In Amazon, there's a big deal about sales tax and everybody should do it, nobody wants to do it and we actually have resources that help people do it. But if I got on a webinar and say, bonus number four, I'm gonna show you how to figure out this whole sales tax problem once and for all, right? I've literally created an objection that I shouldn't have created and it's not a value add. And so if it's not a, if it's not an, oh my God, I can't believe I'm getting this, it will probably get in the way. Even if it doesn't create an objection, it takes up time to explain it. And so the number one rule that we look at, the corollary to that is if it doesn't help, it hurts, but it's gotta help like, oh my God, the idea is this Russell, if I can explain it or show it in five seconds or less and get that initial, oh my God, or that initial shock factor, then I'm considering it as a bonus. And then anything I can do to unpack that further is gonna be massively beneficial. So in your case with the traffic secrets, the oh my God, is you need traffic, I got traffic for you and because I wanted to give you the best possible solution I could, I was willing to spend a million dollars for you. And their answer is oh my God, that's crazy, I wanna hear more about that bonus. And then after that, it doesn't really matter what you say because you've got them hooked on that bonus. And then each new bonus you add to that, if it also stacks that oh my God, oh my God, oh my God, you're just gonna make sales because of the emotional impulsivity of that person to wanna immediately dive into those bonuses. So that's a really important thing is, it's gotta have that, what I call out a glance test. If somebody could just glance at that, even better if they were muting you on the webinar and they could only see your slide within the first few seconds of seeing that bonus if they were like oh God, I want that, or I need that, or I wanna know more about that. Now you have something to play with. Russell, here's something really important, I wanna expand on this. When we do any really good bonus package even for our own offers, to me it's like an audition. I might audition throughout the course of pitching a product 10, 15, 20 bonuses. And usually I just let the marketplace tell me. If I don't see that visceral reaction, if I don't see people getting excited about it, then we keep the bonus in the members area for the client but we just pull it out of the pitch and pull it out of the landing page and we try a new bonus until we get the combination of bonuses, because I mean I've been doing this for a while but even I, half the time I'm just guessing when I'm putting a bonus out there. So I'm putting it out there, seeing what happens, pulling it back, still keeping it as a real value add and then putting another bonus in its place. It's funny, when I'm doing this, similar thing trying to figure stuff out, I will stealthily go to people in my office and I was like, hey, think about putting this in as a bonus. And I'm just watching, because if Dave's like, oh, that sounds awesome. Like crap, that's the wrong thing. If he jumps up, he's like, what? Oh, I start freaking out, I'm like, okay, that's the right. And I'll go ask three or four people and see their emotion and I don't tell them I'm testing, I'm just asking, like, hey, we'll just think about putting this in there. Just to see, because I wanna get the same response from them as the market's gonna give me. Because yeah, you're right. Sometimes you share something. It's funny, because when I first did the click funnels pitch, I knew people wanted like copywriting stuff, right? And so like, we had these scripts and stuff and it was never like sexy. And then Jim Edwards and I built funnel scripts and I took me like two years to convince them finally, it's like, okay, we can put funnel scripts in and I pay them in a row with each one now. We put it in and first time I was like, oh, you saw the funnel scripts and build your copy. And again, same thing, it wasn't like, we didn't get this big profound impact. And so again, this is for the 10 X events. After meeting you, spoke and I was the same thing. How do I make this sexy? How do I start doing it? And so I instead of sharing it, I went back and I figured out the whole story behind it and talked about how it's really like amplified the story behind this thing to decrease the desire so that when I was like able to reveal it, it was huge. And I told the whole story behind funnel scripts and how Jim went through, you know, a hundred years of direct response market and all the top converting headlines every single industry and they took those and rewrote them in a way you can fill in the blanks that puts your product and service and literally have like Gary Halber and Dan Kennedy, all these legends writing your headlines for you and do the same thing for Facebook. And I tell this whole story and it was funny because there was a bunch of inner circle members, my inner circle that were in the audience and had people afterwards that were like, they came up to me like, I have owned funnel scripts for five years. Every year I pay the monthly fee or whatever, the yearly fee and they're like, that literally was getting me out of my seat to go buy. And I was like, I already have this, but I still want it because like the way you pitched it is suddenly like, I want to go re-log in now and start using it because it reminded me how exciting it was. Like that's how much desire you have to amplify into each of these bonuses otherwise, it just kind of just falls by the wayside. Yeah, and then it gets in your way. So oftentimes we remove bonuses, not ad bonuses, because we'd rather have three or four really tight bonuses that we could just articulate the full story on than 10 or 12 or 15 bonuses that are just, you know, eh, you have seven out of 10, right? Yeah. So what do you tell people who are like, but I don't have any bonuses, Jason, I don't know how to make my own thing, I don't know, those kind of things that happen. I say good because then I'll make the money that you would otherwise make. I mean, come on, this is work here. This is high paid, high value work. So if you're looking at it from, I don't have the bonuses instead of what does my audience need to really make the difference, then you're selfish and you shouldn't make the sale, right? So that's the reframe on there, first of all. But the reality is if you don't have the bonuses, there's a lot of tricks that you can utilize. Russell and I are two of the only guys that really use the trick of sourcing other people's material for our bonuses. Most of the time that's where I immediately tried to do before doing anything else. So I'll give you an example, right? So I was, I was just, I recently was having breakfast with this guy, Chris Voss. So Chris Voss wrote this book, Never Split the Difference. Right now it's one of the top 20 best selling books on Amazon. I think he sold a couple million copies of this thing. And it's all about negotiations and it's really powerful. What I could do is pay him his hourly rate. And say his hourly rate is like $1,500 or $2,000, right? Great, pay him and interview him. And if 90% of his information is the same information you have in the book, but it's all focused around what is related to the offer. So like let's say you're teaching webinar closing. We could say this guy's an FBI, former FBI top negotiator in the world. He sold over 2 million books. He's one of the best sellers right now. People pay him, big corporations pay him $25,000 a day for an onsite training. And what I did is I took his material, paid him his rate, sat down with him and interviewed him specifically of how to apply this to this situation because I know now you're gonna have a technique or a method that you'll never find anywhere else. Because I went out there and found it for you. This doesn't exist, nobody's ever done that. So what's the value of this? The value of this is $2,500. Now Russell, imagine if you were selling a course for $199, but you had a bonus, that was $2,500. Why is it? Because somebody else would have to pay that. And then take it a step further. You say, I will never distribute this recording in public. I won't charge for it. I won't give it away for free. I will never put it outside of this product. Now it's legitimately the only way somebody could get that is if they pay this guy $2,500 and knew what questions to ask. And now it's free and you're like, oh my God, I'm getting a $2,500 value for free by buying a $199 product, that doesn't make sense. And the real question then is, is it worth your time to pay something like that? Or you could sit down and beat it up and do it yourself. But one of the best things about a bonus is the proof attached to it. If you could start saying things like, top 20 Amazon bestseller currently, sold over 2 million books, top FBI negotiator, negotiated some of the most tense negotiations on a worldwide stage. You get paid $25,000 a day for his information. And what I did is I focused it specifically on this one topic. You take the hottest thing going on right now. He's one of the hottest authors right now. And he's got everything going for him. And it cost you what, $2,500? And now you have a bonus. You got to sell what, 10, 15 of your product to break even on that? It's crazy, right? Or what we'll do, so here's another one for you, right? We'll go to a client that sells a product for like $2,000 or $3,000 or $5,000 or maybe even $10,000, right? And we'll negotiate a deal to take a portion of that product and put it in our product. And people will do this all the time, Russell, because the argument is always free advertising. Listen, what's it cost you to generate a lead? It cost me this, right? Well, what if I could give you a buyer lead for free by giving them some of your material first and then showing them where they could go if they want more of you? Now you're not paying for any advertising. It's just coming to you. People are very amenable to that. And so if you could take a portion of somebody else's product and negotiate with them to put it in your product, then you could say the only way you would ever see this bonus was if you bought so-and-so's product over there for $20,000 or $10,000 or $5,000. But I specifically thought it was so important that you know this piece of information from this legendary person who's accomplished X, Y, Z and who's doing A, B, and C that I went out there and negotiated for you on your behalf to include it. And the lesson we should talk about this positioning real fast, Russell, is it's always I went to bet for you. Customers respond very positively to when you say, listen, I thought this was important. You have this. So I took it on my own shoulders. I put on my own effort to get it for you. That communicates to your customers that you are truly working for them towards the result. And that probably is just as effective in terms of the sell as the actual bonuses. That's awesome. It's interesting. What you're so good at and that I model as well as like, what you're looking for is you're looking for the story, right? Like we can last time we talked, you talked about how you got Trey to interview it. And I was like, why did you pick Trey? And you're like, well, Trey right now has the number one grossing, high escorcing funnel and click funnels and as an e-commerce funnel selling flashlights. I'm talking to, so it's like, I'm able to use this, like you're paying for the story more so than anything. At the time that this, we were recording this interview right now, we launched the 10 X secret course yesterday. And if you look at the very first upsell, basically what is John Childers, who I initially were in public speaking from, who's been retired for 10 years. Like I interviewed him and then afterwards I said, hey, can I license your old training? You no longer sell anywhere. He said, yes, we license that. And like that's the upsell, but it's the story behind like, this is what people paid to be at this training. This is all the speakers he trained how like the hundreds of millions of dollars have been generated because of this training. And I went and I bought the license. It's no, you can't buy it anywhere. He's retired. Boom. So like I'm paying for the story as much as the content and the thing. And so it's just like, for you, if you're like, I created my product, it's done, but I don't, I don't know what the bonuses are going to be. It's like, okay, what are the stories you can leverage? People in your market, people who are in, in tangential markets who maybe it's not doing web, like he said, they're not teaching webinars, but they're teaching FBI negotiation. Like how can I take that person and bring them over? That person probably is a lot cheaper than hiring Jason to talk about webinar selling. You know what I mean? Cause like, you know what your value of it and stuff like that. So it's like, it's like looking for the story. The John Reese was the story, funnel scripts and the store ban funnel scripts. Like the story is what you're paying for. Cause that's what causes the desire and increases the perceived value of whatever it is that you're actually offering. Cause you're like, I interviewed this guy named Trey about his Ecom funnel. That, you know, if the interview is exactly the same, the value is because of the story. Like that's what you're, that's what you're, you're paying. Yeah. I go, this is the number one transaction guy in click funnels. So if anybody would know about building funnels for Ecommerce products, which was an audience we served, right? It would be this guy. And I don't even have to convince them anymore. I don't have to get into the details and explain it and justify it and do this and do that with it. I just, boom, kill shot, right? Once that ends and it's done. Nobody can, nobody can. I think that I want people understand is like, is in Utah basis, like you know Trey, you could just call him to try and phone the interview and find, but you're like, no, like, let me wire you money. It needs to be wired. So I need a screenshot of the wire to show everyone like, this is how much it costs to get this. Cause now the increased value, the perceived value once again goes up. Oh, absolutely. And again, and it also communicates to the audiences. I'm not asking you to do anything that I don't do. In fact, I do it at a level that's much bigger than what you do it at. So I was speaking at that Shanda Sumter's event early this year. You know Shanda, right? Oh yeah, I meant Shanda through you. When we were at Pirate's Coke. I need a percentage of that commission. Whatever you made. Well, I didn't make anything. I helped her sell her product for free just cause I like to sell so much. So she sells this $5,000 thing and there was like a lunch the next day for all the people who bought the day before and she asked me to come in and say some words. And so Shanda and I and Russell, although I don't know if he ever shows up, we're all part of a hundred K mastermind with Joe Polish and called Genius X. And so this is what I said to the audience. These are people that just paid, I think it's a five grand or I think it's 15 K, something like that, right? It doesn't even matter, right? And I asked everybody in the audience, I said, is this the biggest investment you've ever made in terms of furthering yourself in business? And probably Russell, 90, 95% of their hands went up, right? And I said, and I laughed at them. I said, this investment for Shanda and I is like a rounding error because we don't invest 15 grand. We invest a hundred grand. So you're a little $15,000 investment, that's a rounding error. And then I took it a step further. I said, now most of you when you're following this program, what are the prices of the products you're gonna sell? And they're like about $1,000, $500 or $1,000 or $2,000. So now I said, your best advantage is tell your audience, listen, I invested $15,000. So you investing $500 is nothing. For the same reason I position their $15,000 investment as nothing compared to my $100,000 investment, I'm not asking you to do anything I haven't done already 10 times over. And people will respond very positively to that. You show them, if I spend money to further my education, then you should as well. But if you don't show them that you're spending money and you're asking them to do something that they don't think you would do, that puts a massive barrier to the close. And what's weird is though, Russell, is we all spend money. We spend a lot of money, all of us do. But if you don't show it, that's the key word, you have to show it. They don't believe you unless you show it. Once you show it, if you can show I spent $10,000, I'm only asking you to spend $500 relative pricing. Nobody can compare price to itself. Let me give you an example of this psychology, right? Is if you said to somebody, hey, listen, this book is $20 here, but if you go down the street, it's only $10. How likely are you to go down the street to get the book and save $10? To go from $20 to $10? The answer is very likely. If you said this Lamborghini is $1,000,000 and $10, but if you go down the street, it's just $1,000,000, right? You are super unlikely to go down the street because you compare price. So you say $20 to $10, that's a 50% off. But $1,000,000 and $10 to win $1,000,000, that's like a 0.1% off. But Russell, here's the punchline. It's still $10. If you would do it in the first scenario to save $10, you would do it in the second scenario to save $10. But psychologically, people don't behave that way. They compare price to other prices relative because they can't visualize numbers in their head. The brain doesn't work that way, right? So when we compare price, when we say I spend $2,500 to further myself in this area, and I'm only asking you to spend $200, what they look at that is, oh my God, I'm just getting 90% off, not, oh my God, look at how much expensive this is to buy this thing, right? So good. Guess the orange Jason on this interview, he's freaking brilliant. I love how your mind works. That's awesome. All right, we're halfway through this, and I haven't even asked you the questions that came in to ask you. So I might take a little detail, but this is amazing, because I think we've covered affiliate stuff, offer stuff, positioning, like there's so many good things that've already happened, and I'm gonna transition a little bit because there's a couple of things I really wanna get into with you as well, that I think will be super beneficial. So, then the first, probably 10 years or so of me doing my stage presentations, and this is the way I learned from most of the people I studied that I modeled as I was learning this game, right? It was like the first five minutes of the presentation, it was important, I said, grab me, you gotta hook him, get excited, then for the next like 55 minutes, you teach him whatever you want, it doesn't even matter, and at the end, you already closed the crap out of him, and so that's what I did for decades, and I thought that was everything, and I remember this is probably, man, five or six years ago now, I got your webinar book, there was like 47 bucks, something crazy, and I was working on a webinar that was like happening the next day, and I just downloaded it to see if I get any ideas, I started reading, and the section in the middle, you talked about this concept, that like it shattered my beliefs about things, and then like, I literally rewrote my webinar that night, like I wish you could have seen, like I had printed out your e-book, all of my desk, I was like changing my slides, I'm like, oh, and I was like, I don't know if you remember how to do this it's so different, but like, it made sense, and so I did it, and that webinar just crushed it, and I remember like a year or two later, we were sitting in a car, I think it was the genus network or genus somewhere, and I was like, hey, just so you know, like this one thing I learned from you, like shifted everything, and then I told you what it was, you're like, I don't remember talking about that, and I was like, that was like the most amazing thing, so I want to talk about it because this is where I think most people screw up when they do their presentations, is they have like, the intro, they get excited, then they teach their content, and then they try to sell, and it's like, what I learned from you was during the teaching side, it's not, like all the stuff you do there is what makes the sell easy later, and what you talked about is it's not just like teaching your best stuff, which is what everyone always does, but it was like, it was figuring out what people's false beliefs were, and then like, reframing it, and that was this key, and like, that opened my mind, and now if you've gone through my perfect webinar stuff, you've gone through like, 10X secret stuff that I teach, it's like my whole thought process is like, three secrets, like what's the biggest false you've ever had in reframing, that all came from the concept I learned that night from you, from that, I love you to kind of talk about that, because I think when I started doing that, it made the sell at the end so easy because they were already sold by the time I got to the point where I started asking for money, it just made it so much easier. Yeah, unfortunately, information rarely leads to a transformation in terms of how somebody behaves. Most of us learn things, we know what we should do, we want to do them, but we continue to do the same things that we have habitually created, right? So, an example, if you teach somebody how to make more money or build better funnels, but they're still scared to put themselves out there, you're not gonna get behavior change. They will agree with you, they will say, best webinar ever, oh my God, that was so insightful, right? But they won't buy. You feel like stabs us as presenters, like it was insightful, no. You know, you've done it right when nobody talks about how good the webinar is, you know? Everybody talks about the future and how life is better now that they have your product and it's going to be better, right? So, shifting somebody's behavior patterns, unfortunately, the brain is very easy to generalize and get stuck in loops. So, let me give you my favorite one, is logically speaking, nobody should ever buy any product on a webinar because most people have already bought 99 other products that supposedly were gonna pay off that big promise and they failed 99 times in a row, right? And now, here you are, number 100, you're gonna come on. Why should it be different? This is why their family and friends, I don't consider them negative thinkers. I consider them realists, because they said this is another get rich quickscape or this is another pipe dream that you're gonna have, but you failed the last 99 times. So, the history, the case history points to, and if we're being logical, it points to the fact that you're probably gonna fail again. And this is what somebody faces when they come onto your webinar or any of your sales pitches. And so, we have to show them how this time is going to be different. So, if we're educating for education's sake, we're probably not gonna do that. But if we show them something and say, now can you see how doing this will allow you to be able to achieve things you've never achieved before. And you can say, okay, listen, now that this is no longer a problem, that's just one of my phrases, you show something and you can say, okay, now that this is no longer a problem, how does that change things for you? What can you now do that you couldn't do before? And people now start thinking towards the future. It's not just education, you are literally creating a new belief pattern of what's possible in that person, in the present. So, they're seeing the future, they're seeing themselves acting and operating in a new way, but they're feeling it in the present. And then when you show your offer at the end and you show how it accelerates that, makes it easier, makes it better, makes it more enjoyable, makes it less tedious, et cetera, et cetera, that just is the commitment and consistency principle. They already saw a better future, and now they have two choices, they can either go at it alone, or they can go at it with you. And most people realize, I would rather take that great future and have it appear sooner by paying a little bit of money, as opposed to having it appear later, by not paying anything, which obviously is more costly, because they're gonna pay in time and stupidity. You pay one way or the other, yeah. You always pay one way, so you gotta reframe that. Give me somebody who's average in every single area, average intelligence, average means, average resources, average whatever, but they have desire to change, they have desire to be better. And I will do more with that person than if you give me somebody who has all the intelligence in the world, has all the means in the world, has all the connections in the world, has all the resources in the world, but is only a five out of 10 on the desire scale. And so if we don't have people who are automatically desireists, we have to make them more desireists of the future behaving in a new way than the present behaving in the same way. Interesting. I wonder if you, I'm curious if you do it the same way. So the way I look at this when I'm writing my presentations and my pitches, and I'm looking at it like, if I was to tell you, like for example, this is like my new favorite supplement, I'm obsessed with it, right? So it's awesome. If I came to you and I would think like, what would be the reasons why Jason wouldn't want to buy this? And I start thinking to like, no more want to taste like cough syrup, it's horrible. Number two, it's $30 per bottle. So it's 30 bucks a day for me to take this stuff. So I'm thinking about all these different, the false beliefs that you have, like I'm not going to pay for that. Like it tastes horrible. It's like, I'm thinking about all those false beliefs. And then I'm thinking about, why did I have that same false belief? Cause there's a reason why I'm paying 30 bucks a day to drink this nastiness, right? So what were the reasons? And I try to think like the stories that I now believe that are the reason why I do this. And so then when I get to my presentation, I'm telling stories to try to like, to trump their story, right? Their story is like, oh, 30 bucks. Why would you, why in the world would you 30 bucks? I'm like, the reason I pay 30 bucks a day is because when I take this stuff, all the brain fog completely lifts off my mind. I'm able to focus. I make a hundred times more than 30 bucks a day when I can focus and I have clarity and, you know, things like that. And all of a sudden they're like, oh my God. Oh, that's interesting. I could think that it's different. So that's what I'm looking for. I go on in a completely different way. And so I'm fast. I'd love to hear how you do it. Cause that's what I'm always looking like. What's the story that they have? And what story do I have that Trump's at that's going to break their story down and then give them my beliefs. Like I want to come in and like take my belief and like replace theirs with mine. Right. And you know, if you can always give them a better story than the story they have, I've learned that from Tony Robbins. That's really powerful. It's changed the story and you can change the outcome. Where I would start is, I would say, is the group in the audience that I'm most likely to reach for where I'm going to make my most money and most impact, are they already taking supplements like this or not? Because if they're not, then I have to shift behavior from an off state of no supplements to an on state of any supplements. So I have to make the sale of taking any supplement before I even make the sale of making my specific supplement, the one they take, right? If my audience is most likely to already take supplements that are similar to this, either directly or indirectly, then I have to switch the belief how this supplement is going to be different, better, whatever, than the supplements that they take right now. And that's a, those are two completely separate approaches. If I'm switching somebody from an off state to an on state versus if I'm modifying existing behavior and pointing it in a new direction. And so that's what I look at. So the real question before we ever even would write the pitch is what's the most lucrative market? What's the one we're most likely to get the strangled, hold on, tie up and get an advantage immediately and then put it into a monopoly? Cause I don't like to compete. I like to just win. It's not the competition that excites me. It's the winning that excites me. So that's how we would look at it. And then we would work backwards from there. And so the idea here becomes is because in my mind, it's anybody would do anything if it solved the biggest problem that they have right now. And we see this all the time. We see people that will, you know, and this is something we learn when we're selling Amazon. I could sell the Amazon opportunity to numerologists and options traders just as easily as I could sell it to the diehard internet marketers because numerologists, a portion of them felt that wealth should be showing up in their life based on their numerology or something like that. Right? It's so I could convince them that this is the way to do that. Options traders, they're already putting money in to markets and hoping to get money out. So I have to position Amazon as a market right now they're only putting money into by buying products, but they're not selling products. They could be taking money out. But that was the ability then to switch them from where they're at and I now to where they want to go and then putting my offer in place like that. So with this is I know anybody who's looking for an edge will take a supplement if they're convinced of it. But if we jump right in and go too far too fast, that's gonna be very challenging. I know you ran into this Russell when you started talking about funnels and everybody's immediately was like, what's a funnel? I had to make funnels cool. That was hard. If you can't sell funnel hacking, you can't sell a click funnels until somebody decides I want a funnel. Now most of them already wanted a funnel. They just didn't know it was a funnel. And so you work backwards from the outcome and then you position funnels to be the one that can get them there easier, quicker, sooner, better than any alternative that they're playing with right now. And that's a great sell. So one of the ways that we typically do that is, I believe you call it like casting stones at enemies or something like that. I call it just eliminating the competition. I'm casting stones and you're like shooting them. Well, you know, it's just like, I believe, okay, there's a solution for everybody. Not everybody is gonna be in the market for click funnels. Certainly it is a premium version of a funnel builder. And so a hobbyist who's just kind of starting out, or does another head from the hole in the ground, they're gonna try to do it for free or do it the bare minimum possible. And if that's not important to their business, then we'll never convince them otherwise to go to the next level. Or they're gonna go through the pain of trying to cut corners, which is the tendency of everybody and then finally decide that, okay, now I'm gonna do it for real. But we don't really care because premium solutions are great because we get the 20% of the market that hits 80% of the dollars. And so by positioning premium solutions, we're hitting that audience who's willing to try anything if it can get the payoff. And so the real question would be like, if I was going into a market in this case that was not taking supplements, and I was trying to convince them to take a supplement, I would have to position supplements to something that they're already doing or trying or taking it. So let's just assume that we're going to bodybuilders, right? And we say, listen, you're already in the gym, you're already doing things that are helping you grow into your optimal self, and you're looking for any advantage in the gym that you can get. So if you can work out for an hour, but make twice the amount of gains, wouldn't you do that, right? Okay, but there's gonna come a point, isn't there, where you're gonna hit a brick wall, where you're gonna plateau. These are all things that are within their model of reality, right? And then what do you do? Do you continue to try to do it from the inside out, or do you consider going from the outside in? And then you can even tell them, there's been a time in your life, I'm sure, where a breakthrough that occurred for you came from somewhere completely out of left field, isn't that true? And they're like, oh yeah, it is, right? And so then you have to say to them, listen, what's, swallow appeal, right? What's the worst that's gonna happen? It's gonna taste bad. Well, let me tell you, it will taste bad, just like it's painful in the gym. But you still do it, don't you? And so this is a way of getting a belief for them to accept by comparing it to something that they're already familiar with. If I try to convince them to do something that they're completely unfamiliar with, I don't care how good it is. I could be selling freaking $10 bills for a dollar, and people won't buy them if they believe that the alternative is so different. You know, this is the reason why Infusionsoft, even though it's clearly not the best solution, has such a big presence in the marketplace, because people are like, yeah, I hate it, but... I at least know how to use it, or kinda know how to use it, and if I spend money with somebody else, I'm gonna have to relearn it from scratch, and I don't even know if it's going to do anything that I already can do, right? That's the scaringness. The scaringness isn't, is this thing better? Is this thing have more features? Is this thing more effective? The scaringness is, I'm worried that I am going to spend money somewhere else, spend an ungodly amount of time learning it, just like I did with Infusionsoft, right? And then not be any better off. So that's the false belief that you have to break. You have to break the belief of adoption that this is not going to be painful, it's not going to be so risky, where you spend money, time, and effort to do something where you're unsure of the payoff, right? And so in that particular case, we wouldn't even necessarily sell any feature. I know that sounds crazy, right? But we wouldn't even talk about how it's better. We want to talk about how it can do this faster, simpler, or easier, right? I would show them how, without even trying, utilizing only 1% of the capabilities of the shopping cart or whatever it is, right? How they would already be better off and they wouldn't have to replace their existing solution. They could do it in tandem. And then of course, knowing once they get in, then they will get the experience that will allow them to make their own decision to say, yeah, dude, this was even better than he told me it would be. But that's the belief that we're changing. The belief is I'm scared to go through a process like I just went through five years ago just to barely get something that I'm paying a whole bunch of money for and barely using, but I'm going to continue to use it because I'm scared to do anything different. We bypass that mechanism instead of trying to hit it head on. We just say, yeah, you keep doing what you're doing, but I just want you to do this one thing for me. And if it only took you 10 minutes a day to do this, and that's all you risked, I don't know, is that worth another million dollars potentially to you? And they're like, well, yeah, okay, I'll try that. And now it's trying it. It's not changing something, but once they try it, we're pretty confident that they're going to switch over if that's the case with this offer. So that's another way that we would break belief is where are they at right now? And what's the limitation that's preventing them from going where they want to go and where we know we can get them? And then we just destroy it. My goal, Russell, is to make people feel stupid for feeling the way they feel about their current problem. And so if I can get them to laugh at themselves, that's huge. If I can get them, so we'll make fun of them on the webinar, we'll tease them, we'll joke with them. It's like, we'll do everything we can because it will change how they look at their problem. When you can't laugh at your own problem, it controls you. But the moment you can laugh at your problem, you control it. And so all that we do is switch the mechanism from I'm constrained, I'm controlled by my problem, to I am now empowered to kick my problems butt. And so that's why I joke with people, I go, yeah, you don't want to do this. I mean, it only will take you 10 minutes and it could possibly make you a million dollars, but imagine if it doesn't. You've just wasted 10 minutes of your life. Oh my God, I wouldn't risk anything for that, would you? Right? Why don't we just be the same that we are? And then 15, 20 years from now, we can continue to be the same. That's fantastic, everybody does that. Like everybody, that's the plan. We could do that. Or I don't know, maybe you want to possibly take 10 minutes to try this out. Now that will break every single selling rule you've ever heard. Sourcasm, teasing your audience, making fun of them, but damn it, we'll sell. I won't win awards. That's friend at the end of the day, right? But I will send them to the back of the room or I will send them through droves into the shopping cart online, right? Yeah, I think the interesting thing about that, I think why it works so well in like mass selling, one on one, it's gonna be a little harder if you're like teasing into their face. Oh, I do it all the time. You're, but I think a mass decision because it's like they can laugh at it and they're like, they know other people and this and themselves, it's not brutal because it's like, oh yeah, we're all doing this as opposed to if you're yelling at somebody directly and start crying, but maybe you can do that too. Yeah, I mean, well, again, if they know you care, it's amazing what you can get away with. Yeah. If you just look at it as something, if they just look at it as something that you're trying to do to them to get your own benefits, then you can't do anything. Like I've seen guys come up and speak before me at like virtual conferences and they'll barely sell. They'll sell from the hill, like so timid and people will accuse them of hard selling, right? And then Russell, I will get up there and I will pitch for four hours and I will pull every dirty trick in the book I can to twist their arm and pull that damn credit card out of their pocket, right? And they will thank me for it and nobody will accuse me ever of hard selling and the difference is because when somebody feels that you genuinely are fighting for their result alongside them and even fighting for it harder than they're fighting for it, you can get away with anything to try to help them understand the solution to their predicament. But when you come across, and oftentimes this is the case of people following procedure too thoroughly, right? They're like, okay, well, slide 15 has to do this and slide 22 has to do this because I read this manual over here that says we have to do all these things. Even though you're trying to help the customer, even though it's in your heart and you want to, it becomes way too mechanical. You suck the soul out of the whole pitch. Whereas I just look at my audience and I get upset that they're not changing because I know they can't and I know they should and I know they want to and I know they're trying and it just drives me nuts that something so stupid is probably stopping them from succeeding. And so I go in there and we just destroy that thing. And then they say, okay, this guy means business. This guy truly is pulling all the stops to help me transform into some, the thing that I want to transform into. And that's what'll make this sell. So if you go back to the bonuses, right? The bonuses is this was so important. I felt this was so important for you to have. And you wouldn't have to go and hunt it down and find it on your own and pay money for you. I've already found the best thing. I've already paid for you because I know how important this is going to be for your success. All of that is consistent. All of that is congruent with that positioning of, I'm going to give you a better solution than you can find anywhere else. And I'm proud of that fact. And I'm gonna do everything I can so you can take advantage of that solution. Cause I would hate for you to go buy somebody else's product that is half as good and you're gonna waste your time there when you're already sitting here in front of me. So good. I'm glad to record this. I love like transcribing things cause even your language patterns, how you use stuff. So awesome. All right. We got about, I don't know, not too much time left. I got one more thing I want to talk to you about. This has been really fun. This is awesome. Okay. So last thing I want to talk to you about. So I know you know that I'm a big fan of using Armourmore and Stack. I use Armourmore presentations. One thing that I, another thing I learned from you that's so cool is, is, and now I weave it inside of my stack. So I used to have a stack where I would just like stack, talk about this like stack, do do do. And what you do is you put in all these like little story closes all throughout like your, your closing stuff. I've been like weaving into my stack now that has had a huge impact on, on getting people to, to buy. Like, like the way you structure your, your guarantees and way you like, there's so many pieces like that that I've learned from you. And I don't know if you want to talk a little bit about those or even like riff a couple of them cause they're kind of fun. But I think that's the big thing is that you're doing these little things in there that are, that are, there's keep pushing through the close and pushing the close. And it's like, it's almost like every time you do one, it's just another wave of people are falling down and they're coming in, they're coming in because these little closes that are happening all throughout the actual big closes happening. Yeah. So the idea here is, you know, even if you just take the traditional stack where it's, here's bonus one and now here's bonus two. Now here's bonus one and two and here's bonus three. Now here's bonus one, two and three. And here's bonus four. That's a traditional stack. So you don't just say one, two, three, four. Before you introduce the next bonus, you rerun through very quickly the previous bonuses, right? So one of the things that I've really learned over there that's effective is not saying it the same way every single time. And so every single time I bring up say bonus number two, I will slightly change how I show it off or how I talk about the benefit of it, right? So like, let's just say like bonus number two is some sort of one click solution to help automate, you know, your funnel. So like, you know, you push this in and it'll automatically put a checkbox upsell in place. I don't know. I'm just making something up here, right? So the first time I might just say, you know, one button software to immediately increase your cells conversion by 10%. The second time I go back to that bonus, I might say, I'd say a simple script that will allow you to make money that you forget is coming in because it's so autopilot, right? The third time I might say it a different way, where I might say using the psychology of do you want fries with that done automatically for you, right? The fourth time I might say it a different way. And so the beautiful thing is it's one bonus, but they're hearing it from 15 different angles. Pain, you know, I like to do the pain angle. I like to do the pleasure angle. I like to do the curiosity angle. I like to do small benefit, big benefit. And that way, every single time I'm running it I'm doing this muscle with every bonus, right? So if you take like John Reese's traffic secrets, I could say here's the first guy that ever do a million dollar a day. And now all of his traffic secrets are there for you. The second time I might say, hey, listen, if you don't have traffic, it doesn't matter how good your funnel is. Now you got the best funnel and you got the best traffic secrets. The third time I might say something along the lines of whether it's free traffic or paid traffic, it don't matter if you get a lot of it and it makes you a lot more money than the effort and time and money you put into it, voila, that's what this bonus does for you. The next time I might say it again differently, right? And so I think just doing that alone dimensionalizes the bonus in a way because you're anchoring it and amplifying it. You're anchoring it and amplifying it. Every time they hear the bonus, they remember how cool it was and then you're adding something new to it. So it just keeps getting better and better and better. And then here's something that we do, Russell, that a lot of people don't do is I always save my best bonus for last, for two reasons. One is nobody seems to do that. A lot of people are so scared that they're gonna miss a sale. They're so afraid, Russell, imagine this situation because it doesn't work like this, but most people listening, I guarantee you, they feel this. They're like, oh my God, once I start pitching, everybody's gonna leave, right? If I don't immediately show them how amazing this product is, I'm gonna lose them right away, right? The reality is if they stayed on your webinar for the last hour and a half, it's harder for them to leave than to continue to stay. That's real commitment and consistency. Now the problem is, the reason why people think everybody's gonna run away is because you will see a drop off immediately because there are some people who are allergic to investing in themselves. And that's okay, we don't have to worry about them. So you will see a slight dip, but watch your attendees. You won't see another dip for a very long time after that. And that's true whether it's a four hour pitch, six hour pitch, eight hour pitch. A person in motion is stays in motion, but a person at rest stays at rest. So somebody sitting in front of a computer screen will continue to stay on the webinar. And so what's really good about that is we, a comedian doesn't give their best material to start their routine, right? They always end with their most powerful punchline, their most powerful joke. And that's what we should do as webinar presenters. And so when I do my final stack, when I go back through and say, so you're gonna get this, you're gonna get this, this, this, this, this, and this, and this, and this, and this, and this, and this. But I've saved the best for last. Yes, even better than, and then run through it again, this, this, this, and this. What possibly could I have saved for you? That is so incredible, so powerful. And then you boom, you drop that on. So that last one, you get this extra setup. You get a market as different than everything else. You get to really get them excited to lean in and save what this is. And then make sure it is the one that you think is the best. And what's really cool about that, Russell, is oftentimes the best one is scarce. You say, I can't do this for everybody. So if you've already signed up, congratulations. You've already qualified for this bonus. But this bonus is so powerful, I'm limited in distribution of what I can do with it, okay? So I'm only extending this bonus to everybody else on this webinar. Who signs up before I hit the end webinar button on this? So if you're not sure whether you should sign up or not, it's time for us to have a real conversation. Because once it's done, it's done. Now what have we done? We've left the most powerful scarcity, the most powerful bonus, the most powerful punch, right at the tail end, where somebody has to make a serious decision. Do I do it or do I don't do it? Because time is running out. And that's incredibly powerful. It's also really good too, because you can cut that out of the replay, because it's at the end. It's an easy cut, right? So if people are, if you're using replays, which everybody should, by the way, then you don't hurt the sale and the longevity and the value of what you could do on the replay, but you still get that in that moment optimization. And so these are psychology, timing is everything. You could do the exact same webinar, word for word. But if you switch how you position bonuses and where you put them, if like puzzle pieces, you move them around and stuff, you can get a dramatically different conversion just by sequencing the order in which you reveal things from bonuses and even guarantees too, right? That's that fascinating. All right, we got three more minutes. I got one more thing I want to ask you. So this will be a quick one, but so powerful. So the very first time we needed the funnel hacks webinar to your list, and we do the whole thing, I did my thing and sales are flying in and then we ended. And normally like I end and then I'm like, it's done. And then you jump in and you're like, hey, I also got a couple of questions. And as a person does webinars, I'm like, no Jason, you're gonna kill the sale. And then you got on for another 90 minutes. And I was just like in my head, I'm just like shaking it. Like, no Jason, stop talking. But you did the most fascinating thing. I could tell during the whole presentation, you were taking notes on like every concern you could possibly think someone have. And then you start at this thing, you're like, all right. She probably thinking this, right? And then you knock down that concern. And you're probably thinking this, right? And you knock down like a certain, you get for 90 more minutes. Remember I was just like, I was like, and then it ended. And I was just like, he just killed the momentum to sell. I guarantee it's horrible. And then all of a sudden Dave and Brent all run in and then they're like freaking out. I'm like, what? They're like, dude, sales came in for the next 90 minutes. Like we more than doubled our sales the next 90 minutes. And I was like, what? It's then drive home. I did a whole podcast on this thing about like, you're probably thinking this, right? And then talked about what you just did and how it's affected sales. And then Brandon and Katelyn listened to the podcast two days later that night. And she shocked us on her interview. I just would do with her that night. She was doing a web or the next station webinar or something like that. And so when she got done, she literally sat there for another hour and did, you're probably thinking this, right? Probably thinking this, right? And she doubled her webinar sales as well. And now like we don't ever do a webinar without doing that in there. So love your psychology reason behind that. And now people can use that as a tool because it's insanely good. Yeah. So I learned this from just years of doing it, Russell. As I would say to myself, why are people still on this webinar who haven't bought but they're listening to me? Is there any length or limit to the amount of selling I can do before people get totally turned off? And the answer is there's not. It's crazy, but people will stay there and listen. So when I first started doing this, Russell, I did it completely like, oh, cool, I get audition material. I don't care if anybody buys or not, but hey, it's good practice. And it's not practice at the laboratory. If I get one or two more sales, if I spend an hour and only suck out or eke out one more sale, but it's a thousand dollar product at the time, I was like, that's time well spent, right? But then what I discovered was that even if it's not the answer to the objection that they have, even if it's somebody else's objection that I'm handling, just the fact that I'll stay there and really work through any objection, communicates to that person that this guy's really serious about this product and really thinks it's gonna help me. And so they'll buy based on the answering somebody else's objection, right? But it also allows me to, strong objections you can't answer once. A really powerful objection that will prevent a sale needs to be handled six, seven, eight, nine, 10, 11 times from the introduction to the content portion to the pitch to the quote unquote Q and A at the end, right? And so what I've discovered is we can also get a lot more aggressive during the closed section. And so, I'm not gonna say the first time I introduce price, I'm not gonna say, hey listen, if you needed a kidney transplant tomorrow or you would die, you could probably come up with $2,500, couldn't you? Well, then why can't you come up with $2,500 to invest in this course that you and I both know you want, right? Too much too soon. But the waiting hours or the waiting minutes of the webinar closed, I could get to that point where I could say, hey listen, I'm tired of you making excuses. I'm not going to stand there anymore because I know if you had to have a life saving trance, you know, if you had to get your kidney replaced and you were gonna die in 24 hours, what would you do? So don't tell me it ain't about the money. Is it about the time? Well, listen, you've just spent three hours on this webinar with me. You've got to say it. Tell me it ain't about the time because it's not about the time, right? It's about what we're gonna do together with the time. And then you just keep hammering it and hammering it and hammering it. Well, you're right, Russell. What we do when we go through the webinar, so if I'm presenting it, I literally have somebody on call with me, flagging questions and transforming them into potential areas of objections. And so when I get to the close, I'm not just making it up. We are going through every single thing that we think portions of our audience will have. And this is the last thing I'll say, because I know our time's almost up, is when you're doing mass selling, I think there's always about six groups of people. There's one group of people that will make up like 15% of your potential sales. But they need to hear something that's completely different than the next group of people that will make up the other 15% or the other 15%, right? And so some people are super risk-averse. So we have to sell them in a way that will minimize risk. Other people are all about reward, reward, reward, reward. So we have to sell them in a way that will maximize benefit. Other people, we have to sell based on hardcore trust. And that's the mechanism that makes them want to buy or will stop them from buying. Other people, we have to show them that this isn't going to embarrass them. And that's the big mechanism that we have to get over with no matter what we're selling, right? So essentially I've identified, no matter when you're selling mass market, there's always about six different segments that will have six different wildly different appeals that will determine if they will buy or not. And so if I can go through six and cycle through them over and over again, I am going to hit every single audience, either by brute force, by luck, or by deliberation with the mechanism that's going to really make the difference for them to buy at the end of the day. Cause somebody, there's a lot of people, a Russell that are so scared of making a mistake that they will continue to do things that make them miserable, right? And so at some point in time in the close, I talk about, and I do that whole worst case scenario, we did this in Amazon all the time because people were so scared about pricing their products incorrectly. That was a huge objection. What should I price my product, Jason? I said, listen, here's the great thing about this, right? I want you to attempt to over-pricer product because you'll be surprised probably because people will buy it anyway. But if they don't, here's the thing. I know this is going to be a lot of work, right? It's going to be very hard, but here's what you're going to have to do. If you accidentally over-pricer product, you're going to have to take the five seconds to log in, push a couple of buttons on your computer keyboard and then hit say, can you do that for me, right? And now we've reframed this, making a mistake to, oh, five seconds, I could just change the price. Okay, I'm in, right? It's up, dude, I mean. But that was someone's fee that kept them from doing it and now it's gone. It's gone now, right? And again, all of this is not stuff we can fit into the presentation, but at the end, this is when we just go straight up with each other. People tend to be nice on these webinars, Russell, so what they'll do is they'll answer questions based on the content of the webinar. So they can say, hey, Russell, when you showed this funnel earlier, you had a red button or a green button, you had a red button here, but you had a blue button over there. When should I do red or when should I do blue, right? In my mind, immediately, what will go off is that's not a buyer question. Whether I answer that question or not, that person isn't going to buy, right? And so I'll either just ignore it, which is preferable, or I'll change it. I'll say, what I'm really hearing is this. You want to make sure that you can do the least amount of work to make the most amount of money. And then I'll close on that, right? Or what I'll do is I'll point blank, say, hey, listen, our time is limited here. And why I like to answer every single question, I'm going to start by priority. And in my mind, the priority is you've sat here, you've listened to this, you're considering the investment. So I want to start with questions that will help you to determine if this is the right thing for you. And then if we have any time left over, we can get to some of the other stuff right now. But what I really want to know for you right now is what would make this a 10 out of 10 for you to know that you should buy this immediately? And what's the question I have to answer to make that so? Right? And I mean, if you listen to these language fatterns, it's like, if you just copy my language fatterns and nothing else, you will make more money, period, right? But the strategy behind them is we force them because it's do or die. You either speak up or you forever hold your peace. But unfortunately, if you don't speak up, your chances of staying the same and suffering even more is almost 100%. And so that's where the out is, time's running out. So we got to get down to business. So we close, close, close, close, close, close, close. Russell, these days I've made so much money, I often quit early, because I could stay on and beat up another hour or two and probably pull another five grand out, right? I want everybody to get to that position to where they will willingly turn around and leave with five or 10 grand still on the table because after three hours, they've had enough. It's amazing. Jason, thank you so much for sharing all this stuff. There's so much golden interview. It's insane from how they're gonna affiliate, positioning of offers, how to break and reframe belief patterns to how to hit all the different angles of every single bonus every time you restack it, which I've never heard you say before.
